title: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Controller CVSS 10.0 Authentication Bypass Zero-Day: Threat Actor UAT-8616 Exploits CVE-2026-20127 Since 2023 for Global Network Infrastructure Takeover, Triggering CISA Emergency Directive ED 26-03 and Five Eyes Joint Advisory

sources.name: CISA Alert - Ongoing Global Exploitation of Cisco SD-WAN
CISA Supplemental Hunting Guidance for ED 26-03
Canadian Centre for Cyber Security Advisory AL26-004
Cisco Talos - UAT-8616 SD-WAN Exploitation Campaign
Tenable - CVE-2026-20127 Zero-Day Auth Bypass Analysis
Rapid7 - Critical Cisco Catalyst Vulnerability Exploited in the Wild
Qualys - Cisco SD-WAN Authentication Bypass Vulnerability
Sophos - Cisco SD-WAN Vulnerabilities in Active Exploitation
Singapore CSA - Active Exploitation of Cisco SD-WAN
The Hacker News - Cisco SD-WAN Zero-Day Exploited Since 2023
BleepingComputer - Critical Cisco SD-WAN Bug Exploited Since 2023

timeline.event: Earliest detected exploitation by threat actor UAT-8616 begins targeting SD-WAN infrastructure globally
Continuous exploitation in the wild with minimal detection, threat actor maintains persistent access across multiple organizations
Cisco, CISA, and Five Eyes alliance (UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand) issue coordinated public disclosure and advisories
CISA issues Emergency Directive ED 26-03 requiring federal agencies to patch within 24 hours
Cisco Talos publishes detailed threat intelligence report attributing attacks to UAT-8616
Federal agency patching deadline at 5:00 PM ET per ED 26-03

simple_summary.what_happened: A maximum-severity vulnerability was discovered in Cisco SD-WAN, the networking platform used by thousands of enterprises and government agencies to manage their wide-area networks. A sophisticated threat actor group called UAT-8616 has been secretly exploiting this flaw since at least 2023 to break into organizations worldwide. The vulnerability allows anyone on the internet to bypass all login protections and gain full administrator access to the SD-WAN management system without needing any credentials. CISA issued an emergency directive requiring federal agencies to patch within 24 hours, and all Five Eyes nations (US, UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand) released coordinated warnings.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: All organizations using Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Controller (formerly vSmart) and SD-WAN Manager (formerly vManage), including both on-premises and cloud deployments. Affected versions span from 20.9.x through 20.18.x. This includes federal agencies, critical infrastructure operators, large enterprises, managed service providers, and telecommunications companies worldwide.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: The attacker sends a crafted HTTP request to an internet-exposed SD-WAN Manager interface on HTTPS port 443, exploiting a broken peering authentication mechanism to bypass all login controls. Step 2: With administrator access obtained without any credentials, the attacker injects a rogue peer device into the SD-WAN control plane topology, establishing persistent network presence. Step 3: The attacker uses the built-in software update mechanism to downgrade the system to an older version vulnerable to CVE-2022-20775, a path traversal flaw. Step 4: Using CLI path traversal commands, the attacker escalates privileges from admin to root, gaining complete system control. Step 5: The attacker creates backdoor accounts mimicking legitimate administrators, installs SSH keys for passwordless access, and configures NETCONF sessions on port 830 to control the entire SD-WAN fabric. Step 6: The attacker purges all logs from /var/log/, clears command history, and restores the software to its original version to destroy evidence of the attack chain.
what_you_should_do: IMMEDIATE (24 hours): Inventory all Cisco SD-WAN Controller and Manager instances, collect forensic artifacts (logs, SSH keys, peer lists) BEFORE patching, and apply security patches to all affected systems

Hunt for indicators of compromise: Check /var/log/auth.log for unexpected vmanage-admin authentications, review control plane peering events for unauthorized peers, audit SSH authorized_keys for rogue entries, and look for evidence of software version downgrades

Implement network hardening: Restrict SSH (TCP/22) and NETCONF (TCP/830) access to known management IPs only, forward all logs to external SIEM with immutable storage, and reset all administrator credentials

If compromise is confirmed: Isolate affected devices, perform complete system reimaging, remove all unauthorized accounts and SSH keys, and conduct full incident response including lateral movement analysis
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: This is one of the most severe network infrastructure vulnerabilities disclosed in recent years. With a maximum CVSS score of 10.0, it requires no authentication, targets a widely deployed enterprise networking platform, and has been actively exploited by a sophisticated nation-state-level threat actor for over three years. Compromised SD-WAN controllers give attackers the ability to intercept, redirect, and modify all network traffic flowing through the enterprise WAN. The CISA Emergency Directive with a 24-hour patching deadline and Five Eyes joint advisory underscore the extreme urgency. Organizations that have not yet patched should assume compromise and begin forensic investigation immediately.

tactics.description: Attacker exploits broken peering authentication in internet-exposed SD-WAN Manager to bypass all authentication controls without credentials
Attacker creates local user accounts mimicking legitimate administrators to maintain multiple backdoor access vectors
Attacker installs SSH public keys in root authorized_keys file for persistent passwordless access
Attacker deliberately downgrades software to exploit CVE-2022-20775 path traversal for root privilege escalation
Attacker purges logs from /var/log/, clears bash history, and restores original software version to destroy forensic evidence
Attacker uses NETCONF sessions on TCP port 830 and SSH to enumerate and control additional SD-WAN fabric devices
Attacker enumerates complete SD-WAN topology including all peer devices, routing policies, and network configurations

detection_guidance.summary: Detection focuses on identifying unauthorized control plane peering events, suspicious authentication patterns, software version anomalies, and evidence of anti-forensic log deletion. Organizations should immediately review SD-WAN logs for any of these indicators going back to at least January 2023.

log_sources.description: Monitor for unauthorized peer connection events showing devices not in the documented asset inventory joining the control plane
Linux authentication log showing SSH and user login events on SD-WAN Controller and Manager appliances
Monitor for unexpected NETCONF connections used by attackers for lateral movement across SD-WAN fabric
Track any firmware or software version changes that could indicate deliberate downgrade for CVE-2022-20775 exploitation
Regular audit of /home/vmanage-admin/.ssh/authorized_keys and root SSH keys for unauthorized entries

what_to_look_for.indicator: Unauthorized peer devices in SD-WAN control plane topology
what_to_look_for.description: Rogue peer injection is the primary persistence mechanism used by UAT-8616 to maintain access even after initial compromise is remediated

what_to_look_for.indicator: Unexpected SSH authentication for vmanage-admin from foreign IPs
what_to_look_for.description: Accepted publickey authentication from IP addresses not in the documented administrator access list
Evidence of deliberate firmware rollback used to enable exploitation of CVE-2022-20775 path traversal

what_to_look_for.indicator: Log file deletion or truncation in /var/log/
what_to_look_for.description: UAT-8616 systematically purges logs and command history to destroy forensic evidence after achieving root access
what_to_look_for.log_field: file_integrity: /var/log/* modified OR deleted outside maintenance

what_to_look_for.indicator: New local user accounts created during non-maintenance windows
what_to_look_for.description: Attacker creates accounts mimicking legitimate administrator names to blend in with normal operations

immediate_actions.action: Inventory all Cisco SD-WAN Controller and Manager instances
immediate_actions.description: Identify all vManage and vSmart instances across on-premises and cloud deployments. Document IP addresses, current software versions, and exposure level (internet-facing vs. internal only).

immediate_actions.action: Collect forensic artifacts BEFORE applying patches
immediate_actions.description: Preserve /var/log/auth.log, entire /var/log/ directory, /home/vmanage-admin/.ssh/ contents, SD-WAN control plane logs, and peering event history. Evidence may be destroyed by patching or attacker cleanup.

immediate_actions.action: Apply security patches to all affected SD-WAN systems
immediate_actions.description: Upgrade to patched versions: 20.9.8.2+, 20.12.5.3+, 20.12.6.1+, 20.15.4.2+, or 20.18.2.1+ depending on current version train. Prioritize internet-facing vManage instances.

immediate_actions.action: Hunt for indicators of compromise across all SD-WAN devices
immediate_actions.description: Search for unauthorized peers in control plane topology, unexpected SSH keys, suspicious vmanage-admin authentications from foreign IPs, and evidence of software version downgrades going back to January 2023.

immediate_actions.action: Implement log forwarding and network access restrictions
immediate_actions.description: Forward all SD-WAN logs to external SIEM with immutable storage. Restrict SSH (TCP/22) and NETCONF (TCP/830) to documented management IPs only. Reset all administrator credentials.
